entities.name: TECHNOLOGY ADOPTION
entities.description: The process by which farmers incorporate new agricultural technologies into their farming practices

entities.name: YIELD
entities.description: The amount of crop produced per unit area, often used as a primary measure of technology success

entities.name: ETHIOPIA
entities.description: The country where the study on agricultural technology adoption was conducted

entities.name: PANEL DATA
entities.description: Longitudinal data collected over three waves used for statistical estimation

entities.name: CORRELATED RANDOM COEFFICIENT MODEL
entities.description: The statistical model used to estimate returns and analyze adoption decisions

entities.name: CHICKPEA
entities.description: The specific crop for which improved technology adoption was analyzed

entities.name: COSTS
entities.description: The financial expenditures associated with the production and adoption of agricultural technology

entities.name: PROFITS
entities.description: The net financial gain resulting from the adoption of agricultural technology

entities.name: COMPARATIVE ADVANTAGE
entities.description: The relative advantage of individual farmers in adopting technology based on their specific circumstances

entities.name: ECONOMIC RETURNS
entities.description: The overall financial benefits, encompassing yields, costs, and profits, resulting from technology adoption

relationships.description: The study of technology adoption was conducted in Ethiopia
Economic returns are suggested to be more relevant than yields in explaining adoption decisions
Yield gains are often focused on but may be misguided in explaining adoption decisions
The model was estimated using three waves of panel data
The model was used to analyze the adoption of improved chickpea technology
The study focuses on the adoption of improved chickpea technology
Economic returns are calculated in terms of yields, costs, and profits
Economic returns are calculated in terms of yields, costs, and profits
Economic returns are calculated in terms of yields, costs, and profits
Comparative advantage was found not to play a significant role in adoption decisions
